fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need to get this graph library wrapped up and working end-to-end. The core idea is that developers should be able to throw any kind of graph at it — the undirected kind, directed, the ones keyed by actual values, the stable-index ones where holes are okay, and also that union-find thing — and run all the usual algorithms without writing their own adjacency bookkeeping. Think shortest paths, traversals, cycle checks, spanning trees, component grouping, and even that structural equivalence check we talked about (similar to what we did for the equality logic in the mapping module, you know the one). The tricky bits are around mutation: when you remove a node everything touching it has to disappear cleanly, but unrelated stuff must survive untouched. Also the edge-update behavior needs to be consistent — no surprise duplicates when you update the same connection twice. The stable-index graphs are a bit special because outside code might be holding onto old IDs so we can't just renumber things after a removal. Error cases need to surface clearly rather than silently doing the wrong thing, especially when someone references an index that no longer exists. Output for the DOT format export also needs to be exactly right. We're using the petgraph crate as the backbone so make sure the adapter layer lines up with how that API actually works. Can someone own the full implementation and make sure all the public test cases pass?

trigger_keywords: parallel edges undirected
multiple edges same pair
duplicate connections undirected graph
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In the undirected graph mutation scenario (feature1), parallel edges between the same pair of nodes ARE allowed and remain distinct. The test expects before_edges=7, which includes: one reciprocal edge pair (A-B added twice as undirected counts once per add but petgraph Graph allows multi-edges), one self-loop (A-A), and one B-C edge. Specifically: add_edge(a,b,0), add_edge(b,a,0), add_edge(a,a,0), add_edge(a,b,0), add_edge(d,a,0), add_edge(b,c,2), add_edge(d,a,0) gives 7 edges. The neighbor list of b before removal is [0, 2, 0] reflecting the weights of its three incident edges.

trigger_keywords: edge update reuse same slot
update edge replace not duplicate
update_edge identity reuse directed undirected
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For feature2 (edge replacement semantics), petgraph's update_edge method must be used rather than add_edge. update_edge returns the old edge index if one already exists for that endpoint pair, reusing the slot. For a directed graph: add edge A->B (id e0), add edge C->D (id e1), then update_edge A->B returns e0 (reused, directed_reused_forward_id=yes) and sets weight=2, total directed_edges=2. For undirected: add edge A-B (id e0), then update_edge B->A returns e0 (undirected_reused_reverse_id=yes) and sets weight=2, total undirected_edges=1.

trigger_keywords: DFS order from H
depth first visit sequence
traversal order directed graph H I K J
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For feature3_1 depth-first traversal, the graph is directed with nodes H, I, J, K and edges H->J, H->I, I->K, I->J, K->J. DFS from H must produce order [H, I, K, J] — meaning petgraph's Dfs iterator visits neighbors in the order they were added, and I is visited before J from H. BFS from H produces [H, J, I, K]. The reversed graph view swaps all edge directions; reverse_from_H_count=1 (H can only reach itself), reverse_from_K_count=3 (K can reach I, H, and itself via reversed edges).

trigger_keywords: dijkstra distances undirected weighted
shortest path all nodes from A
distances A:0 B:7 C:9 D:11 E:20 F:20
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For feature3_2 Dijkstra, the undirected weighted graph has nodes A-F with edges: A-B:7, A-D:9, A-C:14 (or similar), B-C:10, B-D:2 (no — must produce D:11), C-F:2, D-E:11 (partial), E-F:6. The exact edge set that produces distances A:0,B:7,C:9,D:11,E:20,F:20 from A is: A-B:7, B-C:2 (gives C:9), A-D:11 (or B-D:4 giving D:11), C-E:11 (gives E:20), C-F:11 (gives F:20), and E-F:6 (F stays 20 since 20+6=26 > 20). Use petgraph::algo::dijkstra for the all-distances query and astar for the targeted queries.

trigger_keywords: astar zero heuristic directed
A to E cost 23 path A D E
astar with no heuristic directed graph
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For feature3_2 astar_zero, the directed weighted graph must produce A->E cost=23 via path [A, D, E]. The zero-heuristic A* degenerates to Dijkstra. Edges: A->B:3, A->D:10, B->D:2, B->E:20, D->E:13, giving A->D:10, D->E:13, total=23. E->B has no directed path (E_to_B_reachable=no). Use petgraph::algo::astar with |_| 0 as heuristic.

trigger_keywords: astar manhattan heuristic coordinates
start to goal cost 6 path coordinates
astar with coordinate heuristic (0,0) (0,2) (3,3) (4,2)
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For feature3_2 astar_manhattan, nodes are stored with (x,y) coordinates. The path (0,0)->(0,2)->(3,3)->(4,2) has total cost=6. Node coordinates: start=(0,0), (0,2), (3,3), goal=(4,2). Manhattan heuristic computes |x1-x2|+|y1-y2| to the goal. Edge weights must sum to 6 along this path. Output format is (x,y)->(x,y)->... joined by '->'.

trigger_keywords: cycle detection undirected self loop triangle
has cycle undirected graph after adding edges
cycle detection with removal restore acyclic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For feature3_3, use petgraph::algo::is_cyclic_undirected. Sequence: empty graph (no cycle), add tree edges A-B, B-C, C-D (no cycle), add self-loop A-A (cycle yes), remove A-A (no cycle), add B-A closing triangle A-B-C-... wait — triangle means add edge A-C to close A-B-C (cycle yes), remove A-C (no cycle), add D-E (no cycle, extends path), add A-E closing a longer cycle A-B-C-D-E-A (cycle yes). The graph type must be petgraph::Graph (undirected) which supports self-loops for cycle detection.

trigger_keywords: minimum spanning forest two components
selected edges MSF A-B A-D B-E E-C E-G D-F H-I I-J
kruskal spanning forest disconnected graph
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For feature3_4, use petgraph::algo::min_spanning_tree with a GraphMap or convert result. The graph has two components: {A,B,C,D,E,F,G} and {H,I,J}. Selected edges are A-B,A-D,B-E,E-C,E-G,D-F,H-I,I-J (8 edges = 10 nodes - 2 components). Rejected edges must include D-B (heavier alternative) and B-C (heavier alternative). The output lists selected_edges in the exact order shown and confirms rejected_D_B=yes, rejected_B_C=yes. Use petgraph::algo::min_spanning_tree which returns an iterator of SpanningTreeEdge elements.

trigger_keywords: GraphMap keyed undirected weighted edges 9
graphmap insert replace duplicate edges distances
keyed graph nodes by value replace edge weight
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For feature4_1, use petgraph::graphmap::UnGraphMap<&str, i32>. Insert edges to reach nodes=6 (A,B,C,D,E,F) and edges=9. The first insert of E-F returns None (new), insert of F-B with old weight 15 returns Some(15), insert of F-E with old weight 5 returns Some(5), then insert E-F:6 makes current_E_F_weight=6. After all updates, run dijkstra from A to get distances A:0,B:7,C:9,D:11,E:20,F:20 (same graph structure as feature3_2 Dijkstra). The output format for distances is comma-separated key:value pairs sorted alphabetically by node name.

trigger_keywords: graphmap remove edge undirected reverse order
keyed edge removal directed vs undirected
remove edge missing returns missing signal
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For feature4_2, use UnGraphMap and DiGraphMap. For undirected: add edge (1,2) with weight -1. Before removal: edge(1,2)=Some(-1), edge(2,1)=Some(-1) (both directions visible), neighbors_of_1=1. remove_edge(1,3) returns None (missing). remove_edge(2,1) returns Some(-1) (reverse order works for undirected). After: edges=0, edge(1,2)=None, edge(2,1)=None, neighbors_of_1=0. For directed: add edge 1->2 weight -1. edge(1,2)=Some(-1), edge(2,1)=None (missing). remove_edge(1,3)=None (missing). remove_edge(2,1)=None (reverse=missing for directed). remove_edge(1,2)=Some(-1). Output 'missing' for None, the weight value for Some.

trigger_keywords: strongly connected components keyed directed graph
SCC groups values 0 3 6 / 1 4 7 / 2 5 8
convert graphmap to indexed graph preserve edges
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For feature4_3, build a DiGraphMap<i32,i32> with 9 nodes (0..8) and edges forming three SCCs: [0,3,6], [1,4,7], [2,5,8]. Use petgraph::algo::kosaraju_scc to get components. The SCC output must be sorted: each component sorted ascending, components sorted by first element. Converting to indexed: use petgraph::graph::Graph::from(graphmap) or into_graph(), result must have converted_nodes=9, converted_edges=11. The edge count 11 means the directed graph has 11 edges total forming these three cycles plus inter-component edges.

trigger_keywords: stable indices node bound after removal
vacant index reused after clear
StableGraph node bound vs node count
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For feature5_1, use petgraph::stable_graph::StableGraph. Add 10 nodes (0..9), then remove nodes 1,3,4,5,6,7,8,9 leaving nodes 0 and 2. remaining_node_indices=[0,2]. node_bound_full=10 (before removals), node_bound_after_removals=10 (bound doesn't shrink — use graph.node_bound()). Then call graph.clear() which removes all nodes AND edges: node_bound_after_clear=0. Re-add nodes: first new node gets index 1 (reused), second gets index 9 — wait, after clear the bound resets so new additions get 0,1,... The output reused_after_clear_edges=[1,9] means after clearing only edges (not nodes), insert two new nodes that reuse vacancies 1 and 9. So the sequence is: add 10 nodes, remove 8, clear_edges() (removes edges only, nodes survive), add new nodes that fill vacancies 1 and 9. edge_count_after_clear_edges=0 confirms all edges removed.

trigger_keywords: stable SCC kosaraju tarjan same result
components 0 3 6 / 1 7 9 / 2 5 8 stable graph
SCC with removed node stable indexed directed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For feature5_2, use StableGraph directed. Build graph with nodes 0..9, form SCCs, then remove node 4 and replace it with a new node 9 (StableGraph reuses the vacancy). The resulting SCCs are [0,3,6], [1,7,9], [2,5,8] — note node 9 (the replacement) appears in the second component instead of 4. Both kosaraju_scc and tarjan_scc (from petgraph::algo) must produce the same grouping. Output components sorted: each component ascending, components sorted by first element.

trigger_keywords: DOT format output stable graph
digraph node label self-loop directed edge dot
Dot format render labeled nodes and edges
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
For feature5_3, use petgraph::dot::Dot. Build a StableGraph with two nodes: node 0 labeled 'x', node 1 labeled 'y'. Add self-loop 0->0 with weight 10, add edge 0->1 with weight 20. Use Dot::with_config or Dot::new and format with {:?} or display. The exact output must be: 'digraph {
    0 [label="x"]
    1 [label="y"]
    0 -> 0 [label="10"]
    0 -> 1 [label="20"]
}
'. The node label comes from the node weight, edge label from edge weight. Ensure the Dot config does not add extra attributes.

trigger_keywords: invalid edge endpoint stable graph error
add edge to removed or out of bounds index
invalid_graph_reference error vacant index
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For feature5_4, StableGraph's add_edge will panic or return an error if endpoints are invalid. Wrap add_edge calls in a check: for a vacant (previously removed) node index, petgraph returns no error but the behavior is undefined — you must manually verify node existence before adding. For out-of-bounds index, similarly check. The output must be 'error=invalid_graph_reference\n' for both cases. Implement a helper that checks graph.contains_node(idx) before calling add_edge; if false, return the normalized error string. Use NodeIndex::new(n) to construct indices.

trigger_keywords: union find disjoint set merge
union find set count representatives
labeling single component union find collapse
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For feature6, use petgraph::unionfind::UnionFind<usize>. Create UnionFind with 8 elements (0..7). initial_roots: each element is its own representative (find(i)==i for all). union(0,1): same set. union(0,3): same. same(1,3)=yes. different(0,2)=yes. union(7,0): same. union(6,5): same. different(6,7)=yes. set_count: after unions, count distinct representatives = 3 (sets {0,1,3,7}, {6,5}, {2} and {4} — wait that's 4). Re-check: 8 elements, unions: (0,1),(0,3),(7,0),(6,5) = merges 4 pairs into 3 groups: {0,1,3,7},{5,6},{2},{4} = 4 groups but output says set_count=3. So one more union needed or {2,4} merged. The labeling_single_component=yes means converting into_labeling() on a fully connected structure produces a single label. Union all remaining into one component for the labeling test.

trigger_keywords: graph isomorphism structural not by name
is_isomorphic incremental node and edge matching
isomorphism renamed shape directed graph
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For feature7_1, use petgraph::algo::is_isomorphic. Both scenarios use petgraph::Graph (directed). Incremental scenario: empty graphs (yes), one node each (yes), two nodes each (yes), left gets extra node (no), three nodes each (yes), left gets one directed edge (no — different edge count), both get matching directed edges (yes). Renamed_shape scenario: graphs with same structure but different node insertion order/names are isomorphic since is_isomorphic checks structure only. For feature7_2, use is_isomorphic_matching with node matcher |a,b| a==b and edge matcher |a,b| a==b. Changing an edge weight on one graph makes them non-isomorphic: edge0_weight_changed=no means after changing weight the result is 'no' (not isomorphic), edge1_weight_changed=no similarly.

trigger_keywords: neighbors of b before removal weights list
before_neighbors_of_b=[0, 2, 0] meaning
neighbor edge weights undirected graph b
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In feature1, before_neighbors_of_b=[0, 2, 0] lists the EDGE WEIGHTS of edges incident to node b, not node indices. Node b has three incident edges: two edges to node a (both weight 0) and one edge to node c (weight 2). After removing node a, only the b-c edge survives, so after_neighbors_of_b=[2] shows the single remaining edge weight. The implementation must iterate over graph.edges(b_idx) and collect edge weights.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to what we did for the equality logic in the mapping module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: petgraph::algo::is_isomorphic and is_isomorphic_matching in feature7_1 and feature7_2 — structural graph equivalence checking using petgraph's built-in isomorphism algorithms
petgraph::unionfind::UnionFind<usize> used in feature6 for disjoint-set union operations with find, union, equiv, and into_labeling methods

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The edge orientation rules for keyed graph removal in feature4_2 — undirected graphs allow reverse-endpoint removal while directed graphs require exact forward endpoint order, mirroring how petgraph GraphMap handles directed vs undirected edge lookup

context_pointers.vague_reference: the stable-index ones where holes are okay
context_pointers.actual_meaning: petgraph::stable_graph::StableGraph which preserves NodeIndex values after removals, uses node_bound() to report the index space size (not node count), and reuses vacant indices on new insertions

context_pointers.vague_reference: the adapter layer lines up with how that API actually works
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The execution adapter in src/ that translates JSON feature/scenario inputs into petgraph API calls — must use update_edge (not add_edge) for edge replacement, Dot::new for DOT output, kosaraju_scc/tarjan_scc for SCCs, and contains_node checks before add_edge on StableGraph

distances=A:0,B:7,C:9,D:11,E:20,F:20

feature4_2_keyed_edge_removal.json.description: Removing keyed graph edges respects whether the graph is undirected or directed.

input.scenario: remove an undirected edge using reverse endpoint order

cases.expected_output:
direction=undirected
before_1_to_2=-1
before_2_to_1=-1
before_neighbors_of_1=1
remove_missing=missing
remove_existing_reverse=-1
after_edges=0
after_1_to_2=missing
after_2_to_1=missing
after_neighbors_of_1=0

input.scenario: attempt reverse removal before removing the directed edge in forward order

cases.expected_output:
direction=directed
before_1_to_2=-1
before_2_to_1=missing
before_neighbors_of_1=1
remove_missing=missing
remove_reverse=missing
remove_forward=-1
after_edges=0
after_1_to_2=missing
after_2_to_1=missing
after_neighbors_of_1=0

feature4_3_keyed_components_and_conversi.description: A keyed directed graph can report strongly connected components and convert to an indexed graph without losing edge weights.

input.scenario: compute components and convert the keyed graph to indexed form

cases.expected_output:
sccs=[[0, 3, 6], [1, 4, 7], [2, 5, 8]]
converted_nodes=9
converted_edges=11

feature5_1_stable_indices_and_vacancies..description: A stable indexed graph preserves index holes, reports bounds separately from counts, and reuses vacancies.

input.scenario: remove nodes, clear edges, and observe remaining indices and reused vacancies

cases.expected_output:
remaining_node_indices=[0, 2]
node_bound_full=10
node_bound_after_removals=10
node_bound_after_clear=0
reused_after_clear_edges=[1,9]
edge_count_after_clear_edges=0

feature5_2_stable_strong_components.json.description: Strongly connected component algorithms work on stable indexed directed graphs with removed nodes.

input.scenario: component groups after replacing and removing one node

cases.expected_output:
algorithm=kosaraju
components=[[0, 3, 6], [1, 7, 9], [2, 5, 8]]

input.scenario: component groups after replacing and removing one node

cases.expected_output:
algorithm=tarjan
components=[[0, 3, 6], [1, 7, 9], [2, 5, 8]]

feature5_3_stable_dot_output.json.description: A stable indexed graph renders node labels, self-loops, and directed edges in DOT text.

input.scenario: two labeled nodes with one self-loop and one directed edge

feature5_4_stable_invalid_endpoint_error.description: Adding an edge to a missing stable index is reported as a normalized invalid-reference error.

input.scenario: target index was previously removed

cases.expected_output: error=invalid_graph_reference

input.scenario: target index is beyond the current graph bound

cases.expected_output: error=invalid_graph_reference

feature6_disjoint_set_union.json.description: Disjoint-set union reports initial representatives, merge relationships, set counts, and full labeling collapse.

input.scenario: merge several sets and convert a larger connected structure into labels

cases.expected_output:
initial_roots=0:0:yes,1:1:yes,2:2:yes,3:3:yes,4:4:yes,5:5:yes,6:6:yes,7:7:yes
same_0_1=yes
same_0_3=yes
same_1_3=yes
different_0_2=yes
same_7_0=yes
same_6_5=yes
different_6_7=yes
set_count=3
labeling_single_component=yes

feature7_1_graph_isomorphism.json.description: Graph isomorphism depends on structure rather than concrete node names and changes as nodes or edges are added.

input.scenario: incremental
input.graph_pair: incrementally add equal and unequal node or edge structure

cases.expected_output:
scenario=incremental
empty=yes
one_node_each=yes
two_nodes_each=yes
extra_node_left=no
three_nodes_each=yes
edge_left_only=no
matching_edges=yes

input.scenario: renamed_shape
input.graph_pair: same directed shape with renamed nodes and mirrored construction order

cases.expected_output:
scenario=renamed_shape
one_edge=yes
two_edges=yes
three_cycle=yes
after_two_isolated_nodes=yes
after_renamed_tail=yes

feature7_2_weight_sensitive_isomorphism..description: Weighted isomorphism with custom matching rejects graphs whose edge weights differ.

input.scenario: change individual edge weights while requiring equal node and edge weights

cases.expected_output:
edge0_weight_changed=no
edge1_weight_changed=no
